title: 2. I Just Don’t Talk About It | Do We Get to Win This Time?
format: Episode 2 of the Ringer's Vietnam-and-Hollywood documentary. With the studios still ducking the war, filmmakers smuggled it onto the screen as a WWII epic, a Korea comedy, a Western massacre. Then the returning vet became a stock character who might snap at any moment.

segments.heading: The war in disguise
segments.note: Vietnam smuggled in as some other battle

films.title: Patton
films.note: George C. Scott as the WWII general, from a script co-written by Francis Ford Coppola. Nixon screened it repeatedly during April 1970 as he weighed sending troops into Cambodia; critics wondered if the invasion followed the movie.

films.title: The Green Berets
films.note: John Wayne's pro-war film, the one big-studio Vietnam movie, and the exception the episode measures everything against.

films.title: The Charge of the Light Brigade
films.note: British satire of a doomed cavalry charge. CIA officer Frank Snepp watched it on a Saigon rooftop and read it as a metaphor for the hopeless war around him.

films.title: MASH
films.note: Robert Altman's pitch-black Korean War comedy. Fox forced on-screen titles fixing it in Korea because they feared audiences would read it as Vietnam, and Altman admitted it was.

films.title: Johnny Got His Gun
films.note: Dalton Trumbo's WWI drama with Jason Robards as a gravely wounded soldier; Metallica later sampled it in the video for 'One.'

films.title: Soldier Blue
films.note: Ralph Nelson's Western ending in a cavalry massacre of Cheyenne women and children, a stand-in for My Lai; Candice Bergen stars. Walkouts and refund demands at early screenings.

segments.heading: The vet comes home
segments.note: the returning soldier as new screen stereotype

films.title: Brotherhood of Death
films.note: Cheap B-movie about Black Vietnam vets who bring the war home against their town's Klan. Stars first-timer Haskell V. Anderson and ex-NFL receiver Roy Jefferson; a Tarantino favorite. The vet's line, 'I just don't talk about it,' gives the episode its title.

films.title: The No Mercy Man
films.note: Revenge picture: a decorated vet returns to a town overrun by thugs and fights back.

films.title: Dead of Night
films.note: Bob Clark horror, later retitled 'Deathdream.' A soldier killed in action comes home anyway, stilted and murderous, and ends up burying himself.

films.title: The Best Years of Our Lives
films.note: The earlier model: three WWII servicemen readjusting. Harold Russell, who lost his hands in the war, played the sailor Homer and won two Oscars for a role he'd lived.

films.title: In a Lonely Place
films.note: Bogart as veteran-screenwriter Dixon Steele, a noir vet who can't be trusted: the untrustworthy-outsider template.

films.title: Slaughter
films.note: Jim Brown as an ex-Green Beret taking on the mob; a classier, sympathetic exception to the killing-machine vet.

films.title: Targets
films.note: Peter Bogdanovich's clean-cut vet turned sniper, ending in a drive-in shootout; re-released after 'The Last Picture Show' made him a hit.

films.title: The Visitors
films.note: Elia Kazan drama, one of the early films casting Vietnam vets as violent, potentially murderous brutes.

films.title: Welcome Home, Soldier Boys
films.note: Same stereotype: returning vets who turn violent.

films.title: Taxi Driver
films.note: Scorsese and Paul Schrader's Travis Bickle, maybe a Marine, maybe lying, with Vietnam hanging over the whole city. The film that made Scorsese a name.

films.title: Rolling Thunder
films.note: John Flynn's revenge picture, a Schrader script rewritten by Heywood Gould. William Devane as a POW who loses his family and his hand; Tommy Lee Jones as his fellow vet. Screenings so gnarly moviegoers threatened the executives.

Ads: Netflix FIFA World Cup Launch Edition

The Charge of the Light Brigade.title: The Charge of the Light Brigade
